questions.question: What is the time complexity of accessing an element in an array by index?
questions.explanation: Array index access is O(1) because arrays store elements in contiguous memory, allowing direct offset calculation.

questions.question: Which data structure follows the LIFO (Last In First Out) principle?
questions.explanation: A Stack follows LIFO — the last element pushed is the first one popped.

questions.question: What does `Array.prototype.push()` return in JavaScript?
options: The new length of the array
questions.explanation: push() adds elements to the end of the array and returns the new length.

questions.question: Which sorting algorithm has the best average-case time complexity?
questions.explanation: Merge Sort has O(n log n) average and worst-case complexity, making it one of the most efficient general-purpose sorting algorithms.

questions.question: In JavaScript, what method removes the last element from an array?
questions.explanation: pop() removes and returns the last element of an array.

questions.question: What is the space complexity of a singly linked list with n nodes?
questions.explanation: A linked list with n nodes stores n node objects, so space complexity is O(n).

questions.question: Which data structure is best suited for breadth-first search (BFS)?
questions.explanation: BFS uses a Queue to process nodes level by level, ensuring FIFO order.

questions.question: What is the output of `[1,2,3].map(x => x * 2)` in JavaScript?
questions.explanation: map() applies the callback to each element and returns a new array: [2, 4, 6].

questions.question: Binary search requires the array to be:
questions.explanation: Binary search works by repeatedly halving the search space, which only works on sorted arrays.

questions.question: What is the worst-case time complexity of Quick Sort?
questions.explanation: Quick Sort degrades to O(n²) when the pivot is always the smallest or largest element (already sorted array).

questions.question: In a binary tree, what is the maximum number of nodes at level k?
questions.explanation: At level k (root = level 0), a binary tree can have at most 2^k nodes.

questions.question: Which JavaScript method creates a shallow copy of a portion of an array?
questions.explanation: slice() returns a shallow copy of a portion of an array without modifying the original.

questions.question: What is a hash table's average time complexity for lookup?
questions.explanation: Hash tables provide O(1) average-case lookups using a hash function to map keys to indices.

questions.question: Which traversal visits the root node first in a binary tree?
questions.explanation: Pre-order traversal visits: Root → Left → Right.

questions.question: What does `Array.prototype.reduce()` do?
options: Maps each element to a new value
Reduces an array to a single value using a callback
questions.explanation: reduce() executes a reducer callback on each element, accumulating the result into a single output value.

questions.question: Which keyword is used to define a class in JavaScript?
questions.explanation: The `class` keyword introduced in ES6 is used to define a class in JavaScript.

questions.question: What is encapsulation in OOP?
options: Inheriting properties from a parent class
Bundling data and methods that operate on that data within a single unit
Creating multiple forms of a method
Hiding the implementation from all other objects
questions.explanation: Encapsulation bundles data (properties) and behavior (methods) into a single unit (class), controlling access.

questions.question: Which keyword is used in a subclass to call the parent class constructor in JavaScript?
questions.explanation: super() calls the constructor of the parent class. It must be called before using `this` in a subclass.

questions.question: What does `instanceof` operator check in JavaScript?
options: If an object is an instance of a specific class or constructor
If a class is a subclass
If a property exists in an object
questions.explanation: `instanceof` checks whether an object is created from a specific class or its parent class.

questions.question: Which OOP principle allows a child class to override a parent class method?
questions.explanation: Polymorphism allows child classes to provide their own implementation of methods defined in the parent class.

questions.question: In JavaScript, what does `Object.create(proto)` do?
options: Creates a new object with proto as its prototype
questions.explanation: Object.create() creates a new object with the specified object as its prototype.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a constructor method in a JavaScript class?
options: To initialize an object's properties when it is created
To inherit from a parent class
questions.explanation: The constructor is automatically called when a new instance is created, used to set initial property values.

questions.question: Which of these is NOT a pillar of OOP?
questions.explanation: The four pillars of OOP are: Encapsulation, Abstraction, Inheritance, and Polymorphism. Compilation is not one.

questions.question: How do you define a static method in a JavaScript class?
options: By placing it outside the class
Using the `static` keyword before the method name
questions.explanation: Static methods are defined with the `static` keyword and are called on the class itself, not instances.

questions.question: What does `#` before a property name mean in a JavaScript class?
questions.explanation: The `#` prefix declares a private class field, accessible only within the class body.

questions.question: Which is the correct way to inherit from a parent class in JavaScript?
questions.explanation: The `extends` keyword is used to create a class that is a child of another class.

questions.question: What is a getter in a JavaScript class?
options: A method that sets a property value
A method that retrieves an object from storage
A special method that gets the value of a property
questions.explanation: A getter (defined with `get`) allows you to access a property with computed or controlled value.

questions.question: What is prototypal inheritance in JavaScript?
options: Objects inheriting directly from other objects via prototype chain
Copying methods from one class to another
questions.explanation: JavaScript uses prototypal inheritance where objects can directly inherit properties and methods from other objects.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes abstraction in OOP?
options: Hiding complex implementation and showing only necessary features
Creating multiple classes from one template
questions.explanation: Abstraction hides complex implementation details and exposes only the relevant interface to the user.

questions.question: What will `typeof class Foo {}` return in JavaScript?
questions.explanation: In JavaScript, classes are syntactic sugar over constructor functions, so `typeof` returns 'function'.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of accessing an element by index in a Python list?
questions.explanation: Python lists are backed by dynamic arrays, so index access is O(1).

questions.question: Which Python data structure uses key-value pairs?
questions.explanation: Python dictionaries store data as key-value pairs and are implemented as hash tables.

questions.question: What does `list.append()` do in Python?
options: Inserts element at a specific index
questions.explanation: append() adds the specified element to the end of the list.

questions.question: Which sorting algorithm is used by Python's built-in `sorted()` function?
questions.explanation: Python uses Timsort — a hybrid of Merge Sort and Insertion Sort — for its built-in sort.

questions.question: What is the difference between a list and a tuple in Python?
options: Lists are immutable, tuples are mutable
Tuples are immutable, lists are mutable
questions.explanation: Tuples are immutable (can't be changed after creation), while lists are mutable.

questions.question: How do you implement a stack in Python?
options: Using a list with append() and pop()
questions.explanation: A Python list acts as a stack using append() to push and pop() to remove the last (top) element.

questions.question: What is `collections.deque` best used for in Python?
options: Implementing queues and double-ended queues efficiently
questions.explanation: deque supports O(1) appends and pops from both ends, making it ideal for queues and deques.

questions.question: What is the output of `[x**2 for x in range(4)]`?
questions.explanation: List comprehension squares 0,1,2,3 → [0, 1, 4, 9].

questions.question: What is the time complexity of checking membership in a Python set?
questions.explanation: Python sets are hash tables, so membership checks (in operator) are O(1) on average.

questions.question: Which traversal of a BST gives nodes in sorted order?
questions.explanation: In-order traversal (Left → Root → Right) of a BST visits nodes in ascending sorted order.

questions.question: What does `heapq.heappush(heap, item)` do in Python?
options: Pushes item onto heap maintaining heap property
questions.explanation: heappush() pushes item onto the heap while maintaining the min-heap invariant.

questions.question: What is the space complexity of recursive DFS on a graph with V vertices?
questions.explanation: Recursive DFS uses the call stack which can go up to O(V) deep in the worst case.

questions.question: How do you reverse a list in Python without creating a new list?
questions.explanation: list.reverse() reverses the list in-place, modifying the original list without creating a new one.

questions.question: What is the purpose of `defaultdict` from the collections module?
options: Provides a default value for missing keys automatically
questions.explanation: defaultdict automatically creates a default value (using a factory function) when a missing key is accessed.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of Merge Sort?
questions.explanation: Merge Sort always divides the array in half and merges, giving O(n log n) in all cases.

questions.question: Which method is automatically called when a new object is created in Python?
questions.explanation: __init__ is the constructor method, automatically called to initialize a new object's attributes.

questions.question: What does `self` refer to in a Python class method?
options: The current instance of the class
questions.explanation: `self` is a reference to the current instance of the class, used to access instance attributes and methods.

questions.question: Which Python keyword is used for inheritance?
options: Parentheses with the parent class name
questions.explanation: In Python, you specify the parent class in parentheses: `class Child(Parent):`

questions.question: What is method overriding in Python?
options: Defining two methods with the same name in one class
Replacing a parent class method with a new implementation in the child class
questions.explanation: Method overriding allows a child class to provide its own implementation of a method defined in the parent class.

questions.question: What does the `@property` decorator do in Python?
options: Allows a method to be accessed like an attribute
questions.explanation: @property allows you to define a method that can be accessed like an attribute, without calling it explicitly.

questions.question: How do you make an attribute private in Python (by convention)?
questions.explanation: Prefixing an attribute with `__` (double underscore) triggers name mangling, making it private by convention.

questions.question: What is `super()` used for in Python?
options: To call the parent class's methods or constructor
questions.explanation: super() gives access to the parent class, commonly used to call its __init__ and other methods.

questions.question: Which of the following represents multiple inheritance in Python?
options: class C inherits A and B:
questions.explanation: Python supports multiple inheritance by listing multiple parent classes: `class C(A, B):`

questions.question: What is the output of `type(42)` in Python?
questions.explanation: type() returns the class of the object. For 42, it returns `<class 'int'>`.

questions.question: What is a class method in Python?
options: A method that can only be called once
A method that takes `cls` as first parameter and is bound to the class
A method that has no parameters
A method defined outside the class
questions.explanation: Class methods use @classmethod decorator and take `cls` (the class) as their first argument instead of `self`.

questions.question: What is a static method in Python?
options: A method that modifies class state
A method that belongs to an instance only
A method that doesn't receive the instance or class as first argument
A method that runs at startup
questions.explanation: Static methods (@staticmethod) don't receive `self` or `cls`, they behave like regular functions inside a class.

questions.question: Which dunder method is called when you use `str(obj)` on a Python object?
questions.explanation: __str__ defines the human-readable string representation of an object, called by str() and print().

questions.question: What is the MRO (Method Resolution Order) in Python?
options: The order in which Python executes methods
The order Python searches parent classes when resolving method calls
The memory allocation order for class instances
The order in which objects are garbage collected
questions.explanation: MRO defines the order in which Python searches classes to find a method during inheritance, using the C3 linearization algorithm.

questions.question: What does `isinstance(obj, ClassName)` check?
options: If obj is exactly an instance of ClassName only
If obj is an instance of ClassName or any of its subclasses
If ClassName is a subclass of obj's class
If obj and ClassName share the same attributes
questions.explanation: isinstance() returns True if obj is an instance of ClassName or any class derived from it.

questions.question: What is the purpose of `__slots__` in a Python class?
options: To restrict the creation of a __dict__ and limit attributes, saving memory
questions.explanation: __slots__ restricts instances to only the listed attributes, preventing __dict__ creation and saving memory.
